channel.title: Nature Cell Biology
channel.description: Nature Cell Biology publishes peer-reviewed original research of the highest quality in all areas of cell biology with an emphasis on studies that provide insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying cellular processes. The journal’s scope is broad and ranges from cytoskeletal dynamics, membrane transport, adhesion and migration, cell division, signalling pathways, development and stem cells, to molecular and cellular mechanisms underlying cancer. Nature Cell Biology provides timely and informative coverage of cell biological advances.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: A transport-independent role for SLC25A12 in mitochondrial stress signalling
item.content:encoded:
Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 27 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01973-1

Jiang et al. show that SLC25A12 interacts with the mitochondrial protease OMA1, enabling activation of integrated stress response during mitochondrial damage. This role is independent of SLC25A12’s transport activity.
item.dc:title: A transport-independent role for SLC25A12 in mitochondrial stress signalling
item.dc:creator: Liu Jiang
Lan Li
Jialiang Guan
Ying Liu
item.dc:source: Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 2026-05-27; | doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01973-1

item.title: Hairpin herds corralled at the lipid droplet
item.content:encoded:
Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01955-3

Lipid droplets acquire hairpin-containing proteins from the ER, but how they travel to lipid droplets and subsequently accumulate has remained unclear. With single-molecule tracking, a study now shows that persistent connections serve as bridges to lipid droplets, where nanoscale domains corral arriving proteins.
item.dc:title: Hairpin herds corralled at the lipid droplet
item.dc:creator: Laura Listenberger
item.dc:source: Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 2026-05-26; | doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01955-3

item.title: Membrane bridges and nanodomain partitioning govern membrane protein targeting to lipid droplets
item.content:encoded:
Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01963-3

Mizrak et al. use single-molecule tracking to dissect mechanisms of protein targeting to lipid droplets. They show that nanodomain-based confinement drives selective protein accumulation on lipid droplets.
item.dc:title: Membrane bridges and nanodomain partitioning govern membrane protein targeting to lipid droplets
item.dc:creator: Arda Mizrak
Jacob Kæstel-Hansen
Jessica Matthias
J. Wade Harper
Nikos S. Hatzakis
Robert V. Farese Jr
Tobias C. Walther
item.dc:source: Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 2026-05-26; | doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01963-3

item.title: Epigenetic programming by H3K23ac defines lineage fate of Meg3+ haematopoietic stem cells and drives immune ageing
item.content:encoded:
Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01960-6

Wei et al. show that Meg3+ haematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) expand upon ageing and present with an increase in H3K23ac levels observed already at middle age. Inhibiting H3K23ac ameliorates megakaryocyte/platelet and myeloid skewing in aged HSCs.
item.dc:title: Epigenetic programming by H3K23ac defines lineage fate of Meg3+ haematopoietic stem cells and drives immune ageing
item.dc:creator: Ni Wei
Huiwen Zhan
Yujun Deng
Min Liu
Yao Xiao
Yanqiu Gong
Xiaodong Wang
Pengbo Guan
Xiaoxian Lou
Yusi Xie
Yuemeng Wang
Zhonghan Li
Lunzhi Dai
Hongbo Hu
Huiyuan Zhang
item.dc:source: Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 2026-05-26; | doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01960-6

item.title: Tissue organization and morphogen gradients dynamically tune one another
item.content:encoded:
Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01987-9

During embryogenesis, morphogen signalling is known to pattern tissues as they remodel. Using genetic and optogenetic perturbations, together with theoretical frameworks of phase transitions and biochemical networks, we uncovered an autoregulatory feedback loop between morphogen gradients and the tissue material state. This work shows that spatial patterning can originate from tissue-scale physical properties.
item.dc:title: Tissue organization and morphogen gradients dynamically tune one another
item.dc:source: Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 2026-05-25; | doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01987-9

item.title: A kinetics-based model of haematopoiesis reveals extrinsic regulation of skewed lineage output from stem cells
item.content:encoded:
Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 25 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01958-0

Single-cell transplantations of long-term haematopoietic stem cells (HSCs), in-depth phenotyping and mathematical modelling identify reconstitution kinetics as a unifying metric of HSC potency and extrinsic regulation as a driver of HSC lineage bias.
item.dc:title: A kinetics-based model of haematopoiesis reveals extrinsic regulation of skewed lineage output from stem cells
item.dc:creator: Esther Rodríguez-Correa
Florian Grünschläger

item.dc:source: Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 2026-05-25; | doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01958-0

item.title: Stress granules shape ferroptosis in cancer
item.content:encoded:
Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 22 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01950-8

A major obstacle in treating glioblastoma is the remarkable resistance of glioblastoma stem cells to standard-of-care therapies such as temozolomide and irradiation. A new study now reveals that dismantling protective structures called stress granules can make these cells more susceptible to these treatment regimens by unleashing ferroptosis.
item.dc:title: Stress granules shape ferroptosis in cancer
item.dc:creator: Maria Livia Sassano
Patrizia Agostinis
item.dc:source: Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 2026-05-22; | doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01950-8

item.title: Stress granules restrain ferroptosis by sequestering ferritin
item.content:encoded:
Nature Cell Biology, Published online: 22 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41556-026-01953-5

Ge, Wang, Zhao, Guo et al. report a role for stress granules in suppressing ferroptosis by sequestering ferritin upon irradiation and temozolomide treatment in glioblastoma stem cells, which may potentially be targeted for improved efficacy.
item.dc:title: Stress granules restrain ferroptosis by sequestering ferritin
item.dc:creator: Zehe Ge
